skillName: Use formal methods for subtraction of decimals; complement of a decimal (1 − p)
misconceptionSummary: Students misalign decimal points when subtracting, especially when one number is a whole number.

steps.narration: Align the decimal points and pad the shorter number with trailing zeros. Five point three becomes five point three zero zero so both numbers have three decimal places.
Subtract right to left, borrowing as needed. Multiple borrows cascade left — each borrow reduces the next column by one.

The complement of a decimal p is one minus p — the amount needed to reach one. Write one as one point zero zero, pad to match, then subtract normally. One minus zero point three seven is zero point six three.

visuals.label: Pad 1 as 1.00 → subtract → complement fills the gap to 1

steps.narration: Zero point six three. Adding zero point three seven and zero point six three gives exactly one — they are complements.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: subtracting 1 − 0.37 as 1 − 37 = −36. Write 1 as 1.00 first, then subtract column by column to get 0.63.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Always pad a whole number with .00 before subtracting decimals. One becomes 1.00 — then the decimal columns exist and subtraction proceeds normally.
